description: Fix portal data privacy: Change portal functions that fetch ALL records from Firestore to use server-side .where() queries so only the current user's data is downloaded. This prevents other employees' data from being accessible in the browser.
steps: In modules-portal.js, update renderPortalOvertime() to use .get() + client-side filter by userId instead of .where() to avoid potential index issues (currently it uses .where which is fine for single-field)
In modules-portal.js, update renderPortalReimburse() to filter by userId or use .where('nama','==',currentUser.nama) instead of fetching all records
In modules-portal.js, update renderPortalKasbon() to filter by userId or use .where('nama','==',currentUser.nama) instead of fetching all records
In modules-portal.js, update renderPortalKPI() to filter by userId or use .where('nama','==',currentUser.nama) instead of fetching all records
In modules-portal.js, update the renderPortal() dashboard stats to use .where() queries for absensi and daily_tasks (userId), cuti (userId), meeting_invites (targetUser)
In modules-test-kesehatan.js, update loadPortalTestKesehatan() to use .where() query instead of fetching all test records
Run node --check on all modified files to confirm no syntax errors
acceptance_criteria: renderPortalReimburse() only fetches current user's reimbursements from Firestore
renderPortalKasbon() only fetches current user's kasbon data from Firestore
renderPortalKPI() only fetches current user's KPI data from Firestore
renderPortal() dashboard stats use filtered queries where possible
loadPortalTestKesehatan() uses filtered queries instead of fetching all records
node --check passes on all modified JS files
Existing functionality is preserved (same data shown to user)
findings: Changed all portal functions from .get() (fetch all) + client-side filter to .where() (server-side filter) queries. This ensures only the current user's data is downloaded from Firestore. For test kesehatan, used multiple targeted queries (by userId, linkedKaryawan, and nama) as fallbacks instead of fetching all records. Single-field .where() queries do not require composite indexes in Firestore.
